file_name: Yang et al. - 2022 - Dermoscopic image classification of pigmented nevus under deep learning and the correlation with pat.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly used deep learning and image recognition algorithms for feature extraction, segmentation, and classification of dermoscopic images of pigmented nevi. It compared the performance of a fusion classifier (achieving 85.82% accuracy with a linear kernel) against conventional methods, demonstrating the use of AI to obtain diagnostic results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' and describes an original experimental study involving 268 patients with pigmented nevi. It details specific methods including dermoscopy, deep learning image classification, and pathological correlation, and reports original results such as sensitivity (88.65%), specificity (90.26%), and accuracy (85.82%) with statistical analysis (P < 0.05).

title: Dermoscopic Image Classification of Pigmented Nevus under Deep Learning and the Correlation with Pathological Features
journal: Computational and Mathematical Methods in Medicine
brief_summary: This study explored the classification of pigmented nevi (PN) using dermoscopy under deep learning and its correlation with pathological features. A total of 268 patients were randomly divided into an observation group (n=134) processed by deep learning and a control group (n=134) processed by conventional methods. The deep learning approach utilized image recognition algorithms for feature extraction, segmentation, and classification, comparing the performance of various classifiers. The observation group achieved a sensitivity of 88.65% and specificity of 90.26%, with a linear kernel accuracy of 85.82%, significantly outperforming the control group. The results indicated that dermoscopy under deep learning improves diagnostic accuracy and that dermoscopic manifestations correlate with PN types, supporting its clinical application.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused on classifying pigmented nevi (PN) diagnosed by dermoscopy under deep learning. The dataset included 268 patients with PN.
One of the four main pathological subtypes of PN studied. Accounted for 31.72% of cases. Mainly distributed on the face and back.

all_task_types.target: Pigmented nevus (PN) subtypes (mixed nevus, junctional nevus, intradermal nevus, acral nevus)
all_task_types.details: The study performs image classification of dermoscopic images to diagnose and categorize pigmented nevi into four specific pathological subtypes. The paper reports classification metrics including accuracy (85.82% with linear kernel), sensitivity (88.65%), and specificity (90.26%). It compares different classifiers (linear, polynomial, sigmoid kernels) and training methods (feature training, fusion feature training, collaborative training) for this discrete categorization task.

datasets_used.name: Binzhou Medical University Hospital Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of 268 patients diagnosed with pigmented nevi (PN) who underwent dermoscopy and histopathological examination at Binzhou Medical University Hospital between January 2019 and May 2020.

use.training: Images from the observation group (n=134) were analyzed using deep learning algorithms for feature extraction, segmentation, and classification.
use.testing: The study compared the deep learning classification results against pathological diagnoses (ground truth) for the entire cohort to evaluate sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy. The dataset was split into an observation group (deep learning) and a control group (conventional method) for comparative analysis.
use.other: The data was used to explore the correlation between dermoscopic manifestations and pathological features.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze measurement data (expressed as mean ± standard deviation) and compare general conditions between the observation and control groups.
To analyze the distribution of pathological types of different dermoscopic types and compare enumeration data (counts and percentages) between groups.
To determine statistical significance for differences in accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC between different kernel functions and training methods.
Software used to perform the statistical analysis of patient data.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference between the groups (observation vs. control) or among the different training methods/kernel functions regarding the measured parameters (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC, or distribution of pathological types).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference between the groups or among the methods regarding the measured parameters.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A P-value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. The study reported significant differences in sensitivity among three training methods (P < 0.05), significant differences in accuracy and AUC among different kernel functions (P < 0.05), and significant differences in sensitivity and specificity between the observation and control groups (P < 0.05). Differences in general conditions (age, gender) between groups were not significant (P > 0.05).

Linear Kernel SVM.AUC: Significantly higher than polynomial and sigmoid kernels (P < 0.05)

Collaborative Training.sensitivity: Higher than feature training and fusion feature training (P < 0.05)

Binzhou Medical University Hospital Coho.notes: The linear kernel accuracy was significantly higher than the other two kernels (P < 0.05). The sensitivity of collaborative training was significantly higher than feature training and fusion feature training (P < 0.05). The sensitivity and specificity of the observation group were significantly higher than the control group (P < 0.05).

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier optimized with deep learning feature extraction

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Deep learning image recognition algorithm for feature extraction, segmentation, and classification
architecture_details.segmentation_algorithm: Image edge segmentation including preprocessing, denoising, hair/bubble removal, and edge smoothing
architecture_details.fusion_method: Simple fusion algorithm combining feature sets (color, texture, shape)
architecture_details.gamma_parameter: Controlled the shape of the kernel to balance error minimization and maximization

training_details.training_methods_comparison: Collaborative training showed significantly higher sensitivity than feature training and fusion feature training (P < 0.05)
training_details.kernel_performance: Linear kernel achieved the highest accuracy (85.82%) and AUC, significantly higher than polynomial and sigmoid kernels (P < 0.05)

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Classification of pigmented nevus subtypes (Mixed, Junctional, Intradermal, Acral)
classification_or_output_head.best_performing_config: Linear kernel SVM with collaborative training

conclusions: Dermoscopy under deep learning can be applied as a diagnostic method for pigmented nevi (PN) and helps improve the accuracy of diagnosis.
The linear kernel SVM classifier achieved the highest accuracy (85.82%) and AUC, significantly outperforming polynomial and sigmoid kernels (P < 0.05).
Collaborative training yielded significantly higher sensitivity compared to feature training and fusion feature training (P < 0.05).

The observation group (deep learning) demonstrated significantly higher sensitivity (88.65%) and specificity (90.26%) compared to the control group (conventional method) with sensitivity of 85.65% and specificity of 84.03% (P < 0.05).
Dermoscopic manifestations of PN show a corresponding relationship with pathological types, providing auxiliary diagnosis in clinical practice.
The study supports the clinical application of deep learning-based dermoscopy for the classification of pigmented nevi.
important_remarks: Single-center retrospective study from Binzhou Medical University Hospital (China), limiting generalizability and introducing potential selection bias.
Small sample size (n=268 patients, 431 lesions) with significant class imbalance (Intradermal nevus n=43 vs. Mixed nevus n=85), which may affect model robustness for minority classes.
No external validation cohort was used; performance metrics are derived solely from the internal dataset.

Deep learning features were fed into a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier rather than an end-to-end deep learning architecture, which may limit the capture of complex hierarchical features compared to modern CNNs.

The study compares deep learning-assisted diagnosis against 'conventional method' (dermatologist diagnosis) rather than against a gold standard algorithm or independent radiologist panel, making the magnitude of improvement context-dependent.
Data availability is restricted ('available from the corresponding author upon request'), preventing independent replication and verification of results.
No information provided on inter-observer variability among the dermatologists who performed the preliminary diagnostic evaluations.

Potential for data leakage if the same patient's images were used in both training and testing phases, though the paper states random division into observation and control groups without explicitly detailing the train/test split strategy for the deep learning model.
